topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox, stark white against the usual clutter. Subject line: "Appointment Confirmation." My hands began to tremble as I clicked it open. Dr. Elias Vance. The name hit me like a physical blow. Elias. The boy who used to dismantle my lunch money in the seventh grade. Now, he was a licensed therapist. A specialist in trauma recovery. A knot tightened painfully in my chest, squeezing the air from my lungs. I reread the confirmation, the glossy paper feeling heavy and ominous in my grip. I spent the next hour pacing my small apartment, the floorboards groaning under my anxious footsteps. Every time I glanced at the calendar, my stomach lurched. What if he recognized me? What if this was some elaborate, cruel game? I kept checking my reflection in the darkened window, searching for a flicker of recognition, a sign that this was all a terrible mistake.

[story 2]

I watched him from across the coffee shop, a practiced, almost unnerving calm radiating from him. Elias. He was sitting in a comfortable armchair, a notepad open on his lap, listening intently to an elderly woman’s rambling story. My jaw felt tight, my knuckles white where I gripped my lukewarm latte. Every time he turned his head, his gaze swept the room, and for a split second, I felt exposed, as if he could somehow pierce through the layers of my carefully constructed composure. A cold sweat prickled along my spine. I tried to focus on my book, but the words swam before my eyes. The thought kept looping: he knew me. He knew the history, the sharp edges of my past that I had tried so desperately to bury. I abruptly pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against tile sounding deafeningly loud in the sudden silence of my internal panic. I needed to leave, needed distance, needed to disappear before he noticed the tremor running through my hands.

[story 3]

The phone call was excruciating. When Elias’s voice, smooth and measured, confirmed the session time, a dizzying wave washed over me. I stood by the kitchen counter, gripping the phone so hard my fingers ached. My breathing became shallow, quick catches in my throat. It felt like the foundation of my day had just shifted, destabilizing everything. I looked around my living room, at the familiar objects—the worn armchair, the stack of unread books—and suddenly they seemed charged with unspoken significance. I started rehearsing conversations in my head, trying to construct defenses, but the words felt clumsy and inadequate. I kept wiping my palms on my jeans, a nervous, repetitive motion. The anticipation was a physical pressure, a tightening band around my ribs that made every inhale feel labored, as if I were holding my breath just in case the air itself might betray me.
